content.full_text:
F P P C RICHARD CARPENTER
ATTORNEY AT LAW
DEC 15 | 54 PM '76 408 "C" STREET 32 Adler Circle
SACRAMENTO 95825
(916) 444-5780 488-5582

December 14, 1976

Edythe Campbell
City Clerk
City of Berkeley
2134 Grove Street
Berkeley, CA 94704

Dear Edy:

Your letter of November 15 was brought to my attention when I returned
from an extended vacation. Even retirees need a change of scenery and
Canada is beautiful. Hopefully, my delayed reply will not have caused
you any inconvenience.

I am, of course, disturbed by the nature and tenor of your letter be-
cause I do know you, your superior record for cooperation and your
knowledge of your position and its duties. While I have accosted
to some of the barbs from some of my former lobbyist friends who don't
want any regulation, one of the pleasant experiences I have had is to hear
from city officials about the very helpful assistance they have received
from Nan Hamilton and the others in the Technical Assistance Division
and Delbert Spurlock and his staff in the Conflict of Interest Division.
Robert Stern, FPPC General Counsel, has as good a knowledge of the election
laws as anyone in the state and has attended many of the Clerks meetings
with their appreciation always being expressed following the meeting.

The Commission is involved in almost everything the FPPC does. We par-
ticipate in all opinions and regulations and we review any legislation
affecting the Act and every advice letter sent out by the staff. We don't
see the advice letters until after they have been sent, but if we disagree
we have an opportunity at our next meeting to discuss our differences of
opinion because there is a standing agenda item re advice letters. I am
afraid that what has happened may be more my fault than anyone elses be-
cause I thought that I had been looking at everything we do through the
eyes of a city clerk and city attorney. The Commission sponsored a
change in the law to relieve appointed city attorneys of their prosecutorial
duties because they were in the untenable position of having to advise
their city officials of what to do and then prosecute them for following
their advice if it was bad advice. If the law had clearly made you the
filing officer for council members and required you to fine them after
they had followed erroneous advice, I'm sure I would have recommended a
similar change in the law. It seems to me it would be quite awkward for
you to have to fine a council member who didn't have a legitimate excuse
for a late filing.

In reviewing the correspondence you sent no mention is made of the reason
Carole Davis failed to file on time. It may well be that the reason is

[The image appears to be mostly blank/white with only some very faint marks or shadows visible at the top. There is no clearly legible text to transcribe.]

Edythe Campbell
Page Two
December 14, 1976


such that the Commission can waive the fine. Attached is a policy
statement the Commission will consider this week relative to fines
for late filing. I would appreciate your comments and suggestions and
would also like to know why Carole Davis filed late. I'm sure she had
a good excuse or you wouldn't have waived the fine.

Michael Bennett has instructed the staff that hereafter when council
members are notified about late filings and fined that copies of letters
will be sent to the Clerk. This will help avoid any misunderstanding
and encourage the close cooperation between city clerks and our staff
so essential to the successful implementation of the Act.

As you know when the Act first was approved by the voters no provision
was included in Section 91013 to permit the fine to be waived. A Commission sponsored bill revising Section 91013 gave waiver authority to
the filing officer and otherwise clarified that and other provisions of
the Act. It was always our view that the filing officer was the Commission with respect to members of the council filing statements of
economic interests, but a contrary view could have been supported by the
FPPC Instruction Manual of 11/4/75 and the Handbook for Election Officials
published in December 1975. In April of 1976 Section 81005(c) was clarified
to expressly provide that the Commission was the code reviewing body for
purposes of financial disclosure statements prepared pursuant to Article 2,
Chapter 7 of the Act by so-called 87200 filers (mayor, council, manager).
The September 1976 amendment referred to in Bob Stern's letter of September 2
made it possible to file most statements under Conflict of Interest Codes
(Article 3) with the agency office rather than the Commission or the Clerk.
I'm quite certain that Dave Beatty discussed this with the Clerks and that
the League supported it because it would relievethe Clerk in medium and
large cities from being the filing officer for hundreds of economic
statements.

You have complete waiver authority for all city officers and employees who
must file other than 87200 filers and the city will retain the fines imposed on all but that handful of city officials designated by 87200. It
is my understanding that the FPPC charges for those forms which are distributed without charge but
that the FPPC charges for those forms which Clerks are required by the
initiative to furnish. I tried originally to get the Secretary of State's
office to make these available at cost but nothing came of it.

This brings me to my final point and that is that I would like to arrange
a meeting with you and a couple of your fellow Clerks, Bob Stern, Mickey
Bennett and myself to discuss the fines, forms and manuals problems and
any others you might have. I would want Dave Beatty and Del Spurlock to
sit in. We want to encourage the closest possible cooperation between
City Clerks and the Commission and its staff. Perhaps out of this meeting

The image appears to be a blank or nearly blank page with only some faint circular marks or artifacts visible at the top. There is no clearly legible text to transcribe.

Edythe Campbell
Page Three
December 14, 1976


we could eliminate any current misunderstandings and provide for an on-
going small committee of Clerks who would assist in guaranteeing state
and local implementation of the Act without further misunderstanding.

Please let me have your reaction to these suggestions as well as the
reason for Carole Davis' late filing.

Kindest regards.

Sincerely,

Richard Carpenter

FPPC TRANSMITTAL OF CORRESPONDENCE

CORRESPONDENT [handwritten] Ralph Campbell- Beverly City, LARK

DATE 11/17          RE [handwritten] Impreccition & Laft June

COPY TO:  (11/13)

ADMINISTRATION                 CONFLICT                    ENFORCEMENT
___BOB T.                      ✓DBL    ✓DAWN               ✓DOE S.
___LINDA                       ___ED                        ___RICK
___JUDY                        ___KEN G.                    ___BARBARA
                               ___AL                        ___FRED

EXECUTIVE                      LEGAL                       TARA
___DAN                          ✓KEN F.                    ___JOHN K.
✓MICKEY                        ___NATALIE                  ___NAN
___CARLA                        ___TED                      ___GINA
___JEANNE                       ___MIKE                     ___MARY ANN
                                                            ___JOHN G.
[handwritten] Doug Carpenter                                ___JAY
                                                            ___RICH


Mr. Carpenter           , Please:

        Take whatever action you consider appropriate
    ✓   Reply direct by    18-597-946    -cc: [handwritten] Deanie
        Prepare suggested reply and return to _________________
    ___  Coordinate reply with _________________________________

[The image appears to be almost entirely blank/white with only some very faint marks or shadows at the top that are not legible enough to transcribe accurately.]

THE CITY COUNCIL
OF THE

CITY OF BERKELEY
CALIFORNIA

EDYTHE CAMPBELL
CITY CLERK

2134 GROVE STREET
BERKELEY, CALIFORNIA 94704
(415) 644-6480

November 15, 1976


Commissioner Richard Carpenter
California State Fair Political
Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA.  95804

Dear Bud:

I have been having a running difference of opinion with members of your
staff and I attach copies of the full correspondence file on it so you
can see how got to where we are.

I still disagree with the staff interpretation.  The fact that the Com-
mission recognized the dispute existed and solved it by fiat with an
administrative regulation on September 7, may settle the dispute but does
not change my opinion.

The staff position is further inconsistent in that City Clerks have been
expected to do all of the things listed under Section 81010.  I doubt
Sacramento staff is doing all of this and they certainly are not supplying
the forms and manuals; they sell the forms and manuals, which is a different
matter.

If the term "filing officer" really was meant to mean a centralized Sacramento
based Fair Political Practices Commission, I believe the law would have said
Commission.  I further do not believe the framers of the initiative ever in-
tended that all of the fines collected under 91013 would end up in the State
of California General Fund.  If that were the case surely they would not
have included a section providing that funds received be deposited in "...the
general fund of the jurisdiction of which he is an officer."

In any event, if your staff wants all the responsibility and all the costs of
providing forms and, apparently, they do, witness the September 7, admini-
strative regulation, there is little I can do to change that.  What I cannot live
with is the apparent vindictive pursuance of the $40 fine from Carole Davis,
which completely ignores their own declaration that the error was mine.  If I
was mistaken, why is it the staff feels it cannot exercise its authority to
waive the fine?

Bud, you've known me long enough to know I am generally cooperative in inter-
jurisdictional matters.  I have been offended more than once by the high-handed
actions of some of the Commission staff and, particularly, with their total
insensitivity toward the inter-relationships of local elected officials with
their local staff.  They communicate directly with councilmembers and do not
send courtesy carbon copies to local clerks.  They, as in this case, write a

Commissioner Carpenter                -2-                November 15, 1976


letter demanding $40 and the clear implication is its my fault. They make
sure that point is ground in by stating "Mrs. Campbell is mistaken".

As it happens I don't believe Mrs. Davis is going to bear a grudge against
me or complain to the City Manager; but if the staff is treating all local
officials so cavalierly, they certainly are causing some local agony.

The staff sponsored amendment regarding filing of conflict of interest forms
this past year is also indicative of their insensitivity to local problems
and implications. To solve their filing problems they have saddled cities
with a terrible situation with clear cost implications. Had staff been willing to check their ideas out with League of California Cities, that pitfall
could easily have been avoided by making the regulation permissive as it
applied to local jurisdictions.

I would appreciate your seeing if the $40 can be waived in accordance with
Sec. 91013 (a), as, in my opinion, this case clearly meets the criteria
written into the statute.

Further, I'd appreciate, as would many city clerks, any moderation of attitude
on the part of staff. We want to get along, to do the job we've got the best
way we can. However, that job isn't made easier by incidents such as has
occurred here.

Best regards,

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]

                                        Edythe Campbell, City Clerk

    Elijah Rogers, City Manager

Candidates, newly-elected officeholders who were non-incumbent candidates and appointees who have not
yet assumed office must file Form 720. See "Instruction Manual for Statement of Economic Interests –
Form 720."

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 907
Sacramento, California 95804
Phone: (916) 322-5660

FORM 721

Statement of Economic Int _ests
(Government Code Sections 87200 et seq.)

This form is for incumbents re-elected to the same office or office
of the same jurisdiction, officeholders filing anniversary
statements and persons who have left office.

See "Instruction Manual for Statement of Economic Interest – Form 721."


1. Name: _________________________________________________________________________

2. Address: _______________________________________________________________________

3. Office: ________________________________________________________________________
         State of    [ ] County of _________________ [ ] City of __________________
         [ ] California

4. Check the appropriate box:

[ ] I have been re-elected to the same office or office of the same jurisdiction. (File within 30 days
     after your new term begins.)

[ ] This is my anniversary statement. (File within 30 days after the anniversary of the date your term
     began.)

[ ] I left the above-named office as of _________________________________ (Date)
     (File within 30 days of leaving office.)

5. Period covered by the Statement:

_________________________________ to _________________________________

VERIFICATION

I understand that investments or interests in real property owned by my spouse or dependent child in
which they have an interest of greater than $1,000 must be reported, and I have included all such
investments or interests in real property in this statement.

I declare under penalty of perjury that I have used all reasonable diligence in preparing this statement and
to the best of my knowledge it is true and complete.

Executed on _________________________ . 19 __ at ______________________________, California.


                                                    ____________________________
                                                           (Signature)

Note to filing officers: Make and retain copies of all statements filed with you and forward the originals within five days of
their receipt to: The Fair Political Practices Commission, Attention Conflicts of Interest Division, P.O. Box 907, Sacramento,
California 95804.

The image appears to be almost entirely blank or white, with only two small curved marks visible near the top of the page that resemble page number indicators or artifacts. There is no legible text content to transcribe.

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95314

November 10, 1976


Carole Davis
City Councilmember
Berkeley, California

Dear Ms. Davis:

     In June, 1976, we sent notice to you regarding the late
filing of your Statement of Economic Interests. The letter
stated that the Political Reform Act of 1974 provides that
a fine of $10 per day, up to a maximum of $100, shall be
imposed on any person who files a statement after its dead-
line, and that your Statement of Economic Interests was
filed four days after its deadline.

     In response to our letter, Edythe Campbell, City Clerk
of Berkeley, informed us that she was the "filing officer"
and that pursuant to Government Code Section 91013 she had
waived fine because the late filing was not willful.
Ms. Campbell is mistaken in her interpretation of the Political
Reform Act, as noted in the enclosed letter to Ms. Campbell
from Robert Stern the Commission's General Counsel. Regarding
the reference in Stern's letter to a regulation "which the
Commission will hear on Tuesday, September 7, 1976" declaring
that the official that receives the original statement is the
filing officer, the Commission adopted that regulation on
September 7, 1976, and a copy is enclosed for your information.

     This letter is to inform you that the fine previously
imposed by the Commission for late filing is payable. As
noted in our letter in June, you have the option of remit-
ting the amount of the fine, $40, or if a justifiable reason
exists for late filing, you may notify the Commission in
writing of that reason and request a waiver of the fine.

     If you have any questions, please contact Dawn Wiser
of our staff.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]

                                        Michael Bennett
                                        Executive Director

MB:av

Enclosures

cc:  Edythe Campbell, City Clerk of Berkeley

CITY OF BERKELEY
Memorandum

TO     Michael Lawson, City Attorney                    DATE  September 27, 1976

FROM   Edythe Campbell, City Clerk

SUBJECT: ECONOMIC INTEREST STATEMENTS REQUIRED UNDER THE GOVERNMENT CODE

     I am enclosing copies of correspondence with Delbert Spurlock of the
Fair Political Practices Commission dealing with the authority of the
Commission to impose fines on election officials for late filing of
Statements of Economic Interests. Please advise whether you believe
it advisable to protect this latest adoption of regulation.

     I do not believe that this regulation reflects the intention of this
Government Code Section and, further, the Fair Political Practices
Commission intends to put any fines in the State General Fund which,
I believe, is contrary to the way the law is written.

     It may not be worth fighting about, but it is in line with the State
Commission's general practice of assuming authority over local election
matters, and I genuinely object to decisions which further that
centralization.


                                        Edythe Campbell
                                        City Clerk

I can see this appears to be a mostly blank page with only a few small marks or shadows visible at the top of the image. The page is predominantly white/empty space. There is no clearly legible text content to transcribe from this document image.

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 ··· 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95314
September 2, 1976

RECEIVED
SEP 7 1976

Edythe Campbell
City Clerk
City of Berkeley
2134 Grove Street
Berkeley, California 94704

OFFICE OF CITY CLERK

Dear Ms. Campbell:

Thank you for your recent letter to Delbert Spurlock
regarding the question of whether the Fair Political Practices
Commission has the authority to fine city councilmen who have
filed late Statements of Economic Interest.

It is our position that the Commission is the filing
officer in charge of reviewing the statements and of fining
persons who file late.

Section 81005(g) of the Government Code requires city
councilmembers to file a statement with the agency (the city
council) which must forward the original to the code reviewing
body. In amendments to the Act adopted this year by the
legislature and now in effect (SB 1373, Chapter 415), the code
reviewing body is the Commission for the purpose of filing
financial disclosure statements for city council members.
(Section 81005(g)) This bill, which we sponsored, supports
our view that the Commission reviews the Statements of Economic
Interests and fines persons who have failed to file on time
pursuant to Government Code Section 87200.

It is particularly important to note that the original
statement comes to the Commission. Throughout the Act, the
office which receives the original has the obligation to
review the statements and impose the fine. Your office is
acting as a conduit for us when you forward the original state-
ment to our office.

It would be anomolous for each agency to be the filing
officer for its officials for the following reasons:

[The image appears to be a blank or nearly blank page with only two small circular marks or smudges visible near the top. There is no readable text content to transcribe.]

Edytha Campbell
Page Two                                    September 2, 1976


     a. the original is forwarded to another body (the code
reviewing body) which has the responsibility for overall
implementation of Chapter 7 vis-a-vis the agency.

     b. employees of the agency would be put in the position
of filing the head of the agency and reviewing his or her
statement. Obviously, such employees are going to be very
reluctant to do so.

     Finally, we have prepared a regulation (copy enclosed)
which the Commission will hear Tuesday, September 7, 1976.
This regulation declares that the filing officer for these
statements is the officer which ultimately receives and
retains the original report or statement.

     Please feel free to call on me if you have any questions.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Robert M. Stern
                                        General Counsel

DRAFT 1  TP:plh
August 26, 1976

PROPOSED REGULATION

Chapter 2:  Definitions

18227.  Filing Officer (82027, 81005(g).

The filing officer for reports and statements

filed under Government Code Section 81005(g) is the office

or officer which ultimately receives and retains the

original report or statement.
